section.title: Vectors in the plane and in space
section.p: The storm story in the previous section used two kinds of objects. Points told us where something was. Displacements told us how to move from one point to another. Those displacements are the vectors.

subsection.title: Vectors as arrows, displacements, and ordered lists
subsection.p: A drone starts at P=(1,2,1) where the coordinates are measured in meters: east, north, and up. It receives the instruction \mathbf v=(4,-1,3). This means: 4 \text{ meters east}, \qquad 1 \text{ meter south}, \qquad 3 \text{ meters up}. After carrying out the instruction, the drone is at P+\mathbf v=(1,2,1)+(4,-1,3)=(5,1,4).

example.title: The same vector in different places

statement.p: The vector \mathbf v=(4,-1,3) can be drawn with its tail at the point (1,2,1), but it does not belong only there. If the same drone instruction starts at A=(10,0,2), then the endpoint is A+\mathbf v=(14,-1,5). The arrow has moved, but the displacement is the same.
A vector in the plane is an ordered pair \mathbf v=(v_1,v_2). A vector in space is an ordered triple \mathbf v=(v_1,v_2,v_3). The numbers v_1,v_2,v_3 are called the components of the vector.

subsection.p: The zero vector is \mathbf 0=(0,0) in the plane and \mathbf 0=(0,0,0) in space. It means no displacement.

subsection.title: Addition and scalar multiplication
subsection.p: Suppose a kayak moves through still water with displacement \mathbf p=(3,1) during a short time interval. At the same time, the current pushes it by \mathbf c=(1,2). The actual displacement is \mathbf p+\mathbf c=(3,1)+(1,2)=(4,3). Vector addition adds the motions component by component.

statement.p: For vectors in the plane, \mathbf u=(u_1,u_2), \qquad \mathbf v=(v_1,v_2), their sum is \mathbf u+\mathbf v=(u_1+v_1,\;u_2+v_2). If c is a number, then c\mathbf u=(cu_1,\;cu_2). The same rules hold in space: (u_1,u_2,u_3)+(v_1,v_2,v_3)=(u_1+v_1,\;u_2+v_2,\;u_3+v_3), and c(u_1,u_2,u_3)=(cu_1,\;cu_2,\;cu_3).

subsection.p: Geometrically, \mathbf u+\mathbf v is found by placing the tail of \mathbf v at the head of \mathbf u. The combined displacement goes from the first tail to the last head.

figure.caption: Adding vectors by placing arrows head-to-tail.

subsection.p: Multiplying by c stretches or reverses a vector. For example, if \mathbf u=(3,1), then 2\mathbf u=(6,2), \qquad -\mathbf u=(-3,-1), \qquad \frac12\mathbf u=\left(\frac32,\frac12\right). The vector 2\mathbf u points in the same direction as \mathbf u and is twice as long. The vector -\mathbf u points in the opposite direction.

subsection.title: Linear combinations and spans
subsection.p: A city map gives two basic walking instructions: \mathbf e=(1,0), \qquad \mathbf n=(0,1). Here \mathbf e means one block east, and \mathbf n means one block north. Then 4\mathbf e+3\mathbf n=(4,0)+(0,3)=(4,3). So the instruction walk 4 blocks east and 3 blocks north is built from the two basic directions.

statement.p: A vector of the form a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v is called a linear combination of \mathbf u and \mathbf v. The numbers a and b are called coefficients.

subsection.p: The same idea works with more vectors: a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v+c\mathbf w. A linear combination is just a recipe: take some amount of each vector and add the results.

example.title: Two directions may fill the plane

statement.p: Every vector (x,y) in the plane can be written as (x,y)=x(1,0)+y(0,1). So the two basic vectors (1,0), \qquad (0,1) can build every displacement in the plane.

The span of vectors \mathbf u and \mathbf v is the set of all their linear combinations: \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf u,\mathbf v\}=\{a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v : a,b\in\mathbb R\}. For one vector, \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf u\}=\{a\mathbf u : a\in\mathbb R\}.

subsection.p: The word span means all the places these directions can reach if we are allowed to scale them and add them.

subsection.title: Lines through the origin and planes through the origin
subsection.p: Start with one nonzero vector in the plane: \mathbf u=(2,1). Its span is \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf u\}=\{t(2,1):t\in\mathbb R\}. Writing out a few values gives the points in .

table.title: A few points on the span of (2,1).

figure.caption: The span of one nonzero vector is a line through the origin.

subsection.p: In space, one nonzero vector also spans a line through the origin. Two genuinely different directions can span a plane. For example, \mathbf u=(1,0,0), \qquad \mathbf v=(0,1,0). Then a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v=a(1,0,0)+b(0,1,0)=(a,b,0). This is the xy-plane: z=0.

definition.title: Lines and planes through the origin

subsection.p: The condition not multiples of each other matters. If \mathbf u=(1,2,0), \qquad \mathbf v=(2,4,0), then \mathbf v=2\mathbf u. The two vectors look like two directions, but they are really the same direction with different lengths. Their span is still only a line, not a plane.
This is our first encounter with redundancy. Later we will give it a name: linear dependence.

subsection.title: Affine lines and affine planes: translating a span
subsection.p: So far, every line and plane passed through the origin. Most lines and planes do not.

A trail in space starts at the campsite P=(1,2,0) and follows the direction \mathbf u=(2,0,1). A point on the trail has the form P+t\mathbf u=(1,2,0)+t(2,0,1). For example, t=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1,2,0), and t=3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1,2,0)+3(2,0,1)=(7,2,3).
The expression P+t\mathbf u means: start at the point P, then move by the vector t\mathbf u.

statement.p: The affine line through P in the direction \mathbf u\neq \mathbf 0 is P+\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf u\}=\{P+t\mathbf u:t\in\mathbb R\}.

If \mathbf u and \mathbf v are not multiples of each other, the affine plane through P in the directions \mathbf u and \mathbf v is P+\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf u,\mathbf v\}=\{P+s\mathbf u+t\mathbf v:s,t\in\mathbb R\}.

subsection.p: The word affine means that we have translated a line or plane away from the origin. The direction is still controlled by vectors. The starting location is controlled by the point P.

statement.p: Let P=(0,0,2), \qquad \mathbf u=(1,0,0), \qquad \mathbf v=(0,1,0). Then P+s\mathbf u+t\mathbf v=(0,0,2)+s(1,0,0)+t(0,1,0)=(s,t,2). This is the horizontal plane z=2. It has the same directions as the xy-plane, but it has been lifted two units upward.

subsection.p: Vectors let us talk about direction without caring where the arrow is drawn. Points let us anchor those directions to actual places. This is enough to describe many lines and planes, but something is still hidden: the coordinates themselves. Writing (3,2) quietly assumes that we have chosen an east direction, a north direction, and a unit of measurement. Change those choices, and the same geometric vector may get different coordinates. The next task is to understand what coordinates are really doing.
